response_raw:
{
    "mode": "human",
    "topic": "How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.",
    "opinion": "I think the best way to deal with fake content is by being aware of it, but not spending too much time because that might make things worse. As someone who's depressed and has only a short amount of daily exposure, I don't want to get caught up in all that negativity online anyway. It’s like when you clean your apartment, there are always new messes to deal with—same thing on social media.\n\nFirst off, you have to understand what fake content is without getting overwhelmed by it. For instance, my cousin used to post pictures of himself running marathons every day from 5 AM until midnight, but he actually works sedentary hours delivering food and has two young kids to care for at home while he's out working long hours.\n\nSo maybe start by questioning the source or checking if it makes sense with your own knowledge. If something seems off—like an inspirational quote that doesn't fit my low energy days, I just ignore it after reading once. And because agreeableness isn’t super high in me (64 out of 100), but still moderate enough to get along sometimes anyway.\n\nAlso helpful might be not scrolling endlessly when you notice misinformation; instead bookmark or screenshot what concerns you so you can reference later without having to keep looking at it. That’s my method: snap a picture once, then leave the feed alone until tomorrow if needed.",
    "reasoning_summary": "I started by considering how awareness helps but limits time exposure due to depression and low extraversion (only 8). Then linked personal experience with questioning sources without deep engagement because of moderate agreeableness. Finally suggested minimal action like saving key info rather than continuous battle against fake content for reasons tied to mental health.",

response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.
response_parsed.opinion:
I think the best way to deal with fake content is by being aware of it, but not spending too much time because that might make things worse. As someone who's depressed and has only a short amount of daily exposure, I don't want to get caught up in all that negativity online anyway. It’s like when you clean your apartment, there are always new messes to deal with—same thing on social media.

First off, you have to understand what fake content is without getting overwhelmed by it. For instance, my cousin used to post pictures of himself running marathons every day from 5 AM until midnight, but he actually works sedentary hours delivering food and has two young kids to care for at home while he's out working long hours.

So maybe start by questioning the source or checking if it makes sense with your own knowledge. If something seems off—like an inspirational quote that doesn't fit my low energy days, I just ignore it after reading once. And because agreeableness isn’t super high in me (64 out of 100), but still moderate enough to get along sometimes anyway.

Also helpful might be not scrolling endlessly when you notice misinformation; instead bookmark or screenshot what concerns you so you can reference later without having to keep looking at it. That’s my method: snap a picture once, then leave the feed alone until tomorrow if needed.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: I started by considering how awareness helps but limits time exposure due to depression and low extraversion (only 8). Then linked personal experience with questioning sources without deep engagement because of moderate agreeableness. Finally suggested minimal action like saving key info rather than continuous battle against fake content for reasons tied to mental health.
response_parsed.tone: Skeptical yet practical, grounded in real-world experiences with some metaphorical language
